claims.text: GAA c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was the second most common variant globally in Group A (classic infantile Pompe disease, onset ≤12 months with cardiomyopathy; n=27) and the most common variant in Asia-Pacific Group A (n=26), but was not frequently reported in other geographic regions.

citations.quote: The variant c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region.

claims.text: In the Pompe Registry, c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was reported a total of 42 times across Groups A, B, and C (spanning classic infantile through adult-onset phenotypes).
c.1935C>A was also reported in Asia-Pacific patients in Group B (n<5; onset ≤12 years without early cardiomyopathy) and Group C (n=7; onset >12 years).
c.1935C>A is listed in Table 5 as one of the Group A (classic infantile Pompe disease) variants in exon 14, indicating it is a variant associated with the most severe disease phenotype.
c.1935C>A was found in homozygosity in 7 CRIM-positive Group A patients (classic infantile Pompe disease with cardiomyopathy onset ≤12 months).

c.1935C>A was also found in homozygosity in Group B patients (onset ≤12 years without cardiomyopathy in first year of life); upon careful review, these patients appear to have had cardiomyopathy reported in the Registry after 12 months of age, suggesting misclassification.

citations.quote: homozygous substitution (missense) variants c.1843G>A, c.1933G>A, and c.1935C>A, typically found in classic infantile Pompe disease patients, were reported in both Groups A and B (Table 6). Upon careful review, the patients in Group B appear to have had cardiomyopathy reported in the Registry after 12 months of age

claims.text: In Asia-Pacific patients, c.1935C>A and the pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A are linked in cis on the same chromosome.

citations.quote: c1935C>A also was common in Group A in this region since c.1935C>A and c.1726G>A are linked in cis in patients in the Asia‐Pacific.

claims.text: c.1935C>A is a known (previously published) variant, not classified as novel; variants found in Group A are considered 'very severe' as they are associated with classic infantile Pompe disease (cardiomyopathy, early symptom onset), and homozygosity for Group A variants is expected to lead to this phenotype.
